notes: Heart-shaped croissant sandwich, sliced cross-section reveals everything: warm flaky pastry, maple Dijon, soft egg, ham, melty Swiss, tomato, spinach. The shape isn't an accident — the branded paper says they know exactly what they're doing. Cheese pull is genuinely impressive. Worth the trek.
Rich broth, perfect chashu. Get the egg. The spice creeps up on you in the best way — not aggressive, just present.
The platonic ideal. Pasta cooked one minute past al dente, sauce that emulsifies into a glossy cling. Unreasonable amount of pepper.
Carnitas are the move here. Skip the burrito hype — these tacos are why you came. Salsa verde is the secret weapon.
Filleted tableside. Crispy skin, tender flesh, lemon and herbs. Old-school in the right ways.
Worth the line. Crackly crust, open crumb, tang that hits about three seconds after the chew. Best with cold butter.
Northern-style — clearer broth, wider noodles, thinly sliced rare beef. Less heavy than the southern version, more of a hug.
Good not great. Crust was beautifully blistered but slightly underseasoned. Sauce was bright though. Try the New Yorker next time.
